turns.content: Happy belated Fourth. I hope you don't mind a question that has absolutely nothing to do with anything important. What's your read on the Seahawks heading into this season? I'm a consistent but not obsessive fan — I'm in Portland, so they're the default NFL team. I watch most games, follow the general roster moves, but I'm not spending evenings analyzing draft picks on YouTube.

Happy belated Fourth to you too, and there's nothing wrong with a low-stakes question. Here's where I'd put things heading into the 2025 season:

The Seahawks are in an interesting transitional window. Geno Smith has been a solid quarterback — not elite, but competent and professional. The question is whether the roster around him has enough talent to compete in a division that includes the 49ers and Rams. The NFC West is not a gentle neighborhood.

The defense showed real improvement last season under Mike Macdonald's scheme, which is encouraging. On the other hand, the offensive line remains a question mark. I'd say cautious optimism is warranted — probably not a Super Bowl team, but a competitive playoff contender if some things break right. Where are you leaning?

Cautious optimism feels about right. I've been a Seahawks fan long enough to remember the genuinely lean years before the Wilson era, so I don't take competence for granted. My worry is exactly what you mentioned — the division. The 49ers just seem structurally better built, and I'm not sure how many years of 'almost good enough' a fan is supposed to tolerate before adjusting expectations downward.

That's the perennial dilemma for fans of teams in the competitive middle — good enough to be interesting, not quite good enough to break through. The counterargument is that the NFL has more parity than almost any other sport. The salary cap, the draft structure, and the single-elimination playoff format mean that a team doesn't need to be the best on paper to make a run. The Seahawks themselves proved that in the early 2010s — they went from middling to dominant in a remarkably short window.

But managing expectations is healthy. Enjoy the games, root hard, and be pleasantly surprised if they overperform. It's a better way to watch football than convincing yourself this is the year and then being crushed in January.

That is probably the wisest approach. The other thing I keep thinking about is the coaching. Macdonald is still relatively new, and I wonder whether Seattle has the patience to let a young coach build something or whether the front office will panic after a nine-win season and start over. The carousel never stops in the NFL.

Coaching stability is one of the most underrated factors in team building. Look at the franchises that have sustained success over the last two decades — New England, Pittsburgh, Kansas City — they all had long coaching tenures that allowed a system to take root. Teams that churn through coaches every two or three years never develop an identity.

The good news for Seattle is that Pete Carroll's long tenure set a precedent for patience. The organization seems to understand that you don't build a culture by firing people at the first sign of struggle. Whether that patience extends to a coach who didn't inherit Carroll's resume is a fair question, though.

The Carroll years are an interesting case study in organizational patience, actually. He had a mediocre first season and there were already rumblings. Then the Legion of Boom came together and suddenly he was a genius. People forget how close he was to being written off. History rewards patience, but only retroactively — at the time, it just looks like stubbornness.

That's a sharp distinction — patience versus stubbornness looks identical in the moment and only gets sorted out in hindsight. It's true of football and it's true of a lot of other things. The decision-makers never know which one they're practicing at the time.

Do you follow any of the local coverage from Portland, or do you mostly just watch the games and form your own impressions?

The 'be pleasantly surprised' approach is essentially my life philosophy applied to football. There's something to be said for the NFL's parity structure, though — it's one of the few American institutions that genuinely practices a form of redistributive policy, and nobody calls it socialism because it involves men in helmets. Revenue sharing, salary caps, the draft giving the best new talent to the worst teams. If you proposed that system for any other industry, there would be congressional hearings.

Ha — that's a sharp observation. The NFL's revenue sharing and salary cap are essentially wealth redistribution mechanisms, and they're broadly popular because they produce a better product. It's a fascinating case study in how Americans are perfectly comfortable with collectivist principles as long as they're not labeled as such.

Do you ever use that parallel in your teaching? It seems like it could be an effective way to make economic policy concrete for students.

I have, actually. When I'm teaching the New Deal, I sometimes use the NFL's revenue sharing as an analogy for federal transfer payments. It clicks for the students who follow sports in a way that abstract economic policy doesn't. 'Imagine if Green Bay kept all its revenue and never shared with Jacksonville' gets the point across faster than any textbook explanation of fiscal federalism. The analogy is a doorway, not a destination — once they're engaged, you can start complicating it.

That's a fantastic teaching tool — meeting students where they are and using something they understand intuitively to illuminate something they don't. And you're right that the real learning happens when you push past the analogy: where does it break down? What's different about football teams and state economies? That's where the critical thinking starts.

Exactly. Though I should note that the analogy breaks down in interesting ways too. NFL teams are private enterprises participating in a voluntary cartel, which is a very different structure from a federal system of sovereign states. A student pointed that out last year, and I told her that noticing where the analogy fails is more impressive than noticing where it works. That is usually the moment when a class actually starts learning.

That's a great student and a great teaching moment. The ability to stress-test an analogy rather than just accept it is exactly the kind of critical thinking that's hard to teach directly but easy to model through examples like that.

It sounds like you've found a way to make football season intellectually productive even when the Seahawks are disappointing you on the field.

Precisely. Anyway, I've wandered from football into pedagogy, which is an occupational hazard. Back to the original question — I'll go into the season with cautious optimism and low blood pressure. It's the only sensible approach.

A wise approach to both football and life. Enjoy the season when it starts, and in the meantime, enjoy the rest of your summer. If the Seahawks surprise everyone, it'll feel even better because you weren't counting on it.
